step_by_step_thinking:
All the following are seen in myasthenia gravis except:
A: Ptosis
B: Muscle fatigability
C: Absent DTRs
D: Normal pupillary reflex
Clinical Reasoning:
According to the retrieved evidence, ptosis (option A), muscle fatigability (option B), and normal pupillary reflex (option D) are all characteristic features of myasthenia gravis. Specifically, ptosis and diplopia are among the most common presenting features. Muscle fatigability is central to the pathophysiology—symptoms worsen with activity and improve with rest. Additionally, pupil exams remain unaffected, as stated under "History and Physical" section: “the pupils, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory examinations are normal.” Conversely, absent deep tendon reflexes (DTRs)—listed in option C—are not mentioned anywhere in the evidence as part of the clinical picture. In fact, the literature explicitly states that DTRs are normal in patients with myasthenia gravis. Therefore, absence of DTRs does not occur in typical presentations of the disease.
Answer Choice:
\boxed{C} According to the provided evidence, ptosis (A), muscle fatigability (B), and normal pupillary reflex (D) are hallmark findings in myasthenia gravis. Deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) are specifically noted to be normal in these patients, so their absence would not be expected. Thus, absent DTRs do not represent a finding in myasthenia gravis.
